prompts.label: MongoDB concepts vs SQL
prompts.text:
Compare SQL tables to MongoDB collections and documents for beginners.

- When document model fits
- JSON-like _id field

No installation required in text.

prompts.label: mongosh insert find
prompts.text:
mongosh commands to create db, insert 3 users, find all, find one by filter.

- Basic query operators $gt

Copy-paste examples.

prompts.label: Node mongoose connect
prompts.text:
Connect Node app to MongoDB with mongoose.

- Schema name email, model User, create and find
- Connection string from env MONGODB_URI

Minimal server snippet.

prompts.label: Update and delete operators
prompts.text:
Examples updateOne $set and deleteMany with filter.

- Explain matched vs modified count

mongosh or mongoose.

prompts.label: Embed vs reference
prompts.text:
Teach embedding subdocuments vs separate collection references.

- Example order items embedded in order doc
- When to reference user_id instead

2 scenarios.

prompts.label: Indexes in MongoDB
prompts.text:
Create index on email unique.

- db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true })
- Query explain briefly

Why duplicates fail.

prompts.label: Aggregation pipeline intro
prompts.text:
Aggregation counting orders per user with $group and $sum.

- Sample docs and pipeline stages listed

Beginner readable.

prompts.label: MongoDB Atlas free tier
prompts.text:
Steps to create free Atlas cluster and connect from local app.

- IP allowlist 0.0.0.0/0 warning for learning only
- Put URI in .env

Checklist.

prompts.label: Transactions when needed
prompts.text:
When beginners need multi-document transactions (brief).

- Transfer balance example story
- Prefer single document design first

Conceptual.

prompts.label: Validate with JSON Schema
prompts.text:
Collection validator with required fields name and email.

- mongosh collMod or createCollection validator

One example.

prompts.label: Change streams primer
prompts.text:
Explain MongoDB change streams conceptually versus polling repeatedly.
Mention prerequisites like replica sets and why Atlas often helps beginners.
Describe resume tokens only as "continue after disconnect" vocabulary.
Compare cost and complexity to polling for toy projects.
Give a small mental model: "tailable cursor with benefits" without huge code.

prompts.label: Compound index field order
prompts.text:
Teach how compound index field order impacts which queries can use the index efficiently.
Use an example equality on status plus sort by createdAt.
Relate this to multikey indexes on arrays cautiously.
Advise running explain on slow queries.
Warn that index design is iterative, not guesswork.

prompts.label: Schema validation on collections
prompts.text:
Show how to attach a schema validator to a collection to reject bad documents early.
Discuss trade-offs with schema flexibility.
Mention document versioning patterns as a separate concern.
Compare with Mongoose validation at the app layer.
Explain how to read validation errors when inserts fail.

prompts.label: Bucket pattern for time-series-ish data
prompts.text:
Describe bucketing many small readings into bigger documents (for example hourly buckets).
Compare to one document per reading for write volume and query patterns.
Mention TTL indexes as a companion idea for expiring old data.
Warn about unbounded array growth inside buckets.
Help decide when a SQL warehouse is still simpler.

prompts.label: Multikey indexes for tags
prompts.text:
Explain multikey indexes when indexing array fields like tags.
Show a query that finds documents containing multiple tags.
Warn about index cardinality and memory growth.
Relate to text indexes only as a pointer, not a full lesson.
Advise measuring query plans when tag sets grow huge.

prompts.label: $lookup join-style queries
prompts.text:
Introduce $lookup as a way to join two collections in an aggregation pipeline.
Compare readability and performance to doing two queries in application code.
Mention $unwind when arrays complicate joins.
Warn that joins can be expensive without indexes.
Give a simple "orders + customers" style example story.

prompts.label: Capped collections niche
prompts.text:
Explain capped collections as a fixed-size FIFO-like log.
Discuss when TTL indexes replaced many capped use cases.
Mention tailable cursors as advanced reading.
Warn beginners not to treat capped collections as general storage.
Point to Kafka or proper logs if scale demands it.

prompts.label: Read preference and staleness
prompts.text:
Describe reading from secondaries for scaling reads.
Explain eventual consistency in plain language with a user-visible example.
Relate write concern "majority" as a companion concept.
Warn about "read your own writes" surprises.
Advise defaulting to primary until you truly need secondary reads.

prompts.label: GridFS for large files
prompts.text:
Explain GridFS splitting large files into chunks and metadata collections.
Compare to storing files in S3-style object storage instead.
Discuss when GridFS still makes sense for an all-Mongo stack.
Warn about backup and restore implications.
Mention streams for reading large files efficiently.

prompts.label: mongodump and mongorestore drills
prompts.text:
Walk through taking a dump of a database and restoring to another environment.
Explain why restores should be rehearsed, not left theoretical.
Mention compression and partial dumps for large clusters.
Warn about credentials on the command line.
Connect this to having a real disaster recovery checklist.
